questions.questionText: The normal hemoglobin level in an adult male is:
questions.explanation: Normal Hb: adult males 13-17 g/dL (avg 15), adult females 12-16 g/dL (avg 14). WHO anemia criteria: males <13 g/dL, females <12 g/dL, pregnant <11 g/dL. Hb carries O2 (each gram binds 1.34 mL O2 — Hüfner's constant). Normal Hb types in adults: HbA (α2β2, 96-98%), HbA2 (α2δ2, 2-3%), HbF (α2γ2, <1%). Reference: Guyton — Blood.

questions.questionText: Erythropoietin (EPO) is mainly produced by the:
questions.explanation: EPO is produced mainly by peritubular interstitial cells (type I fibroblast-like cells) of the kidney (~90%) in response to hypoxia (via HIF — hypoxia-inducible factor). Liver produces ~10% (main source in fetus). EPO stimulates erythroid progenitor cells (CFU-E) in bone marrow. CKD → reduced EPO → renal anemia (treated with recombinant EPO). Reference: Guyton — Erythropoiesis.

questions.questionText: The lifespan of a normal red blood cell is approximately:
questions.explanation: RBC lifespan ≈ 120 days. Senescent RBCs are removed by the reticuloendothelial system (mainly spleen — 'graveyard of RBCs'). Hb is broken down: globin → amino acids (recycled), heme → biliverdin → unconjugated bilirubin → liver conjugation → bile. Iron is recycled. Platelet lifespan ≈ 8-10 days. WBC (neutrophil) ≈ 6-8 hours in circulation. Reference: Guyton — RBC Life Cycle.

questions.questionText: The most common blood group in the Indian population is:
questions.explanation: Blood group O is the most common worldwide and in India (~38-40%). Group B is the second most common in India (~33%). Group A (~22%), AB (~7%). Group O individuals have no A or B antigens on RBCs but have anti-A and anti-B antibodies. Universal donor: O negative. Universal recipient: AB positive. Bombay phenotype (hh): no H antigen, extremely rare. Reference: Guyton — Blood Groups.

questions.questionText: Hemolytic disease of the newborn (erythroblastosis fetalis) most commonly occurs due to:
questions.explanation: Rh incompatibility: Rh-negative mother sensitized by Rh-positive fetal RBCs (usually during first delivery) → produces anti-D IgG antibodies → cross placenta in subsequent pregnancy → attack fetal Rh+ RBCs → hemolysis, hydrops fetalis. Prevention: anti-D immunoglobulin (RhoGAM) given at 28 weeks and within 72 hours after delivery. ABO incompatibility is milder (natural anti-A/B are IgM, don't cross placenta well). Reference: Guyton — Rh System.

questions.questionText: The intrinsic pathway of coagulation is initiated by:
questions.optionB: Contact activation (Factor XII with subendothelial collagen)
questions.explanation: Intrinsic pathway: initiated by contact activation — Factor XII (Hageman factor) activated by subendothelial collagen → XIIa → activates XI → XIa activates IX → IXa + VIIIa (tenase complex) → activates X. Extrinsic pathway: tissue factor (from damaged tissue) + Factor VII → VIIa-TF complex → activates X. Common pathway: Xa + Va (prothrombinase) → prothrombin → thrombin → fibrinogen → fibrin. PT tests extrinsic; aPTT tests intrinsic. Reference: Guyton — Coagulation.

questions.questionText: Prothrombin time (PT) is used to assess the:
questions.explanation: PT assesses extrinsic (Factor VII, tissue factor) and common pathway (X, V, II/prothrombin, fibrinogen). Prolonged in warfarin therapy, vitamin K deficiency, liver disease. INR (International Normalized Ratio) standardizes PT. aPTT assesses intrinsic (XII, XI, IX, VIII) and common pathway. Prolonged in heparin therapy, hemophilia A (VIII) and B (IX). Bleeding time assesses platelet function. Reference: Guyton — Coagulation Tests.

questions.questionText: The vitamin K-dependent clotting factors are:
questions.optionB: II, VII, IX, X (and Protein C, S)
questions.explanation: Vitamin K-dependent factors: II (prothrombin), VII, IX, X, and anticoagulant proteins C and S. Vitamin K is needed for γ-carboxylation of glutamic acid residues (allows Ca2+ binding). Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase → reduces functional vitamin K → decreases these factors. Factor VII has the shortest half-life (~6 hours) → PT prolongs first with warfarin. Reference: Guyton — Vitamin K.

questions.questionText: The oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve shifts to the RIGHT with:
questions.optionC: Increased 2,3-DPG, acidosis, increased temperature, increased CO2
questions.explanation: Right shift (Bohr effect — decreased O2 affinity, facilitates O2 unloading to tissues): increased 2,3-DPG, decreased pH (acidosis), increased PCO2, increased temperature. Memory: 'CADET face Right' — CO2, Acid, DPG, Exercise, Temperature. Left shift (increased affinity): opposite conditions + CO poisoning, fetal Hb, methemoglobin. P50 (PO2 at 50% saturation) increases with right shift (normal ~26.6 mmHg). Reference: Guyton — O2 Transport.

questions.questionText: The most potent platelet aggregating agent is:
questions.explanation: TXA2 is the most potent platelet aggregator and vasoconstrictor, produced by platelets from arachidonic acid via COX-1 → thromboxane synthase. Aspirin irreversibly inhibits COX-1 → reduces TXA2 → antiplatelet effect for the platelet's lifetime (8-10 days). ADP is also an important aggregating agent (clopidogrel blocks P2Y12 ADP receptor). Prostacyclin (PGI2) from endothelium opposes TXA2. Reference: Guyton — Platelet Function.

questions.questionText: Heparin acts as an anticoagulant by:
questions.explanation: Heparin potentiates antithrombin III (AT-III) by 1000-fold. AT-III is a serine protease inhibitor (serpin) that inactivates thrombin (IIa), Xa, IXa, XIa, XIIa. Unfractionated heparin (UFH) activates AT-III against both thrombin and Xa. Low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH, e.g., enoxaparin) preferentially inhibits Xa. Monitored by aPTT. Reversed by protamine sulfate. Reference: Guyton — Anticoagulants.

questions.questionText: Reticulocyte count is increased in:
questions.explanation: Reticulocytes are immature RBCs (contain residual RNA, stained by supravital stains like brilliant cresyl blue). Normal count: 0.5-1.5%. Increased (reticulocytosis) in: hemolytic anemia, acute blood loss, response to treatment (iron, B12, folate supplementation) — indicates active erythropoiesis. Decreased in: aplastic anemia, CKD, untreated nutritional deficiency (ineffective erythropoiesis). Reference: Guyton — Reticulocytes.

questions.questionText: Von Willebrand factor (vWF) is important for:
questions.explanation: vWF acts as a bridge between platelets (binds GP Ib receptor) and exposed subendothelial collagen → platelet adhesion (first step of primary hemostasis). It also carries and stabilizes Factor VIII in circulation. Von Willebrand disease (most common inherited bleeding disorder): reduced/dysfunctional vWF → prolonged bleeding time, normal PT, prolonged aPTT (due to reduced VIII). Reference: Guyton — Platelet Adhesion.

questions.questionText: The ESR (erythrocyte sedimentation rate) is increased in all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: ESR is DECREASED in polycythemia vera (increased RBC mass → increased viscosity → slower sedimentation). ESR increased in: inflammation, infection, pregnancy, anemia, multiple myeloma (high immunoglobulins promote rouleaux), malignancy, autoimmune diseases (RA, SLE). Factors increasing ESR: high fibrinogen, globulins, low albumin, anemia. Westergren method is standard. Reference: Guyton — ESR.

questions.questionText: The normal platelet count is:
questions.explanation: Normal platelet count: 1.5-4 lakh/μL (150,000-400,000). Thrombocytopenia: <150,000 (bleeding risk increases significantly below 50,000, spontaneous bleeding <20,000). Thrombocytosis: >400,000. Platelets are fragments of megakaryocytes (produced in bone marrow), lifespan 8-10 days. Thrombopoietin (TPO, from liver) regulates production. 4,000-11,000 is normal WBC count. Reference: Guyton — Platelets.

questions.questionText: Plasmin (fibrinolytic enzyme) is activated from plasminogen by:
questions.explanation: tPA (from endothelial cells) converts plasminogen to plasmin → digests fibrin clot → fibrin degradation products (FDPs), including D-dimer. Streptokinase (bacterial) and urokinase also activate plasminogen. tPA is used in acute MI, ischemic stroke, PE. D-dimer is elevated in DVT/PE, DIC, and other clotting conditions. Aminocaproic acid and tranexamic acid inhibit fibrinolysis. Reference: Guyton — Fibrinolysis.

questions.questionText: The most abundant plasma protein is:
questions.explanation: Albumin is the most abundant plasma protein (~3.5-5.0 g/dL, ~60% of total plasma proteins). Functions: maintains plasma oncotic pressure (~75-80% of total), transports drugs/hormones/bilirubin/fatty acids, acts as a buffer. Total plasma proteins ≈ 6-8 g/dL. Globulins ≈ 2-3.5 g/dL (α, β, γ fractions). Fibrinogen ≈ 0.2-0.4 g/dL. All produced by liver (except γ-globulins by plasma cells). Reference: Guyton — Plasma Proteins.

questions.questionText: Iron is absorbed in the:
questions.explanation: Iron absorption occurs mainly in the duodenum and upper jejunum. Ferric (Fe3+) is reduced to ferrous (Fe2+) by ferrireductase and vitamin C. Fe2+ enters enterocytes via DMT1 (divalent metal transporter 1). Stored as ferritin intracellularly or exported via ferroportin → oxidized to Fe3+ by hephaestin → bound to transferrin in blood. Hepcidin (from liver) inhibits ferroportin → master regulator of iron homeostasis. Reference: Guyton — Iron Metabolism.

questions.questionText: DIC (Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation) is characterized by:
questions.explanation: DIC: pathological activation of coagulation cascade → widespread microvascular thrombosis → consumption of platelets and clotting factors → paradoxical bleeding (consumption coagulopathy). Labs: increased PT/aPTT, decreased platelets, decreased fibrinogen, increased FDPs/D-dimer, schistocytes on blood smear. Causes: sepsis, trauma, obstetric (amniotic fluid embolism, abruptio), malignancy, snake bite. Reference: Guyton — DIC.

questions.questionText: Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) differs from adult hemoglobin (HbA) in that HbF:
questions.optionD: Is found only in the mother
questions.explanation: HbF (α2γ2) has HIGHER O2 affinity than HbA (α2β2) → left-shifted O2-Hb curve. This is because HbF binds 2,3-DPG less avidly (γ chains have lower DPG affinity). Higher O2 affinity facilitates O2 transfer from maternal blood to fetal blood across the placenta. HbF normally decreases after birth (replaced by HbA by 6 months). Persistently high HbF is protective in sickle cell disease and thalassemia. Reference: Guyton — Fetal Hemoglobin.

questions.questionText: The white blood cell with the longest lifespan is:
questions.explanation: Memory lymphocytes (both B and T memory cells) can survive for years to decades, providing long-term immunological memory. Neutrophils: 6-8 hours in circulation, 1-2 days in tissues. Eosinophils: 8-12 hours in blood. Monocytes: 10-20 hours in blood (but tissue macrophages survive months-years). The short lifespan of neutrophils necessitates continuous high production (~10^11/day). Reference: Guyton — WBC Lifespan.

questions.questionText: The normal A:G (albumin:globulin) ratio is:
questions.explanation: Normal A:G ratio ≈ 1.5-2.5:1 (albumin normally exceeds globulin). Reversal of A:G ratio (globulin > albumin) occurs in: chronic liver disease (decreased albumin production), multiple myeloma (increased globulins), chronic infections, nephrotic syndrome (albumin loss). A:G ratio is a simple screening test for liver and kidney disease. Reference: Guyton — Plasma Proteins.

questions.questionText: Blood group antigens are located on:
questions.explanation: ABO blood group antigens are glycolipids/glycoproteins present on the RBC membrane (also on other cells and in secretions in 'secretors'). A antigen → N-acetylgalactosamine is terminal sugar. B antigen → D-galactose. O → no additional sugar (only H antigen). AB → both. Antibodies (isoagglutinins) are naturally occurring IgM (anti-A, anti-B) produced against non-self antigens. Reference: Guyton — Blood Group Antigens.

questions.questionText: Carbon monoxide poisoning causes tissue hypoxia because CO:
questions.optionB: Binds Hb with 200-250x greater affinity than O2, forming carboxyhemoglobin
questions.explanation: CO has 200-250 times greater affinity for Hb than O2. Even small amounts of CO displace O2, forming carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) which cannot carry O2. CO also shifts the O2-Hb dissociation curve to the LEFT (remaining O2 is held more tightly, releasing less to tissues). Cherry-red skin color. Treatment: 100% O2 or hyperbaric O2. PaO2 may be normal (CO doesn't affect dissolved O2). Pulse oximetry is falsely normal. Reference: Guyton — CO Poisoning.

questions.questionText: The site of hematopoiesis in adults is:
questions.optionB: Red bone marrow of flat bones
questions.explanation: In adults, hematopoiesis occurs in red bone marrow of flat bones (sternum, ribs, pelvis, vertebrae, skull, proximal ends of femur/humerus). In fetal life: yolk sac (first 2 months) → liver and spleen (2-7 months) → bone marrow (7 months onward). In severe chronic anemia, extramedullary hematopoiesis can resume in liver and spleen. Reference: Guyton — Hematopoiesis.

questions.questionText: Hepcidin regulates iron homeostasis by:
questions.optionB: Inhibiting ferroportin (blocks iron export from enterocytes and macrophages)
questions.explanation: Hepcidin (produced by liver) is the master regulator of iron homeostasis. It binds to and degrades ferroportin (the only known iron exporter on cell surface) → blocks iron release from enterocytes (reduces absorption) and macrophages (sequesters iron). Increased hepcidin: iron overload, inflammation (anemia of chronic disease). Decreased hepcidin: iron deficiency, hypoxia, increased erythropoiesis. Reference: Guyton — Hepcidin.
